---
title: "Methodology"
---

## 1. Problem Framing

### What this challenge is

VAST Challenge 2025 Mini-Challenge 2 presents a bias investigation framed around a fictional coastal town, Oceanus. The town's oversight board, COOTEFOO, has been accused of economic bias by two opposing activist groups — FILAH (pro-fishing) and TROUT (pro-tourism) — who each submitted datasets as evidence. A journalist, Edwina Darling Moray, has obtained both datasets alongside additional records of her own.

The task is not simply to visualise the data. It is to use visual analytics to determine whether the accusations are supported by evidence, whether that evidence is trustworthy, and what the complete picture looks like once all available records are considered.

### Why it matters

This is fundamentally a problem about the **integrity of evidence**. Both groups make confident accusations. Both groups have data. The question is whether the data was selected honestly or curated to manufacture a narrative. This has direct parallels to real-world government accountability and media bias — making the analytical approach here more than an academic exercise.

The four questions we needed to answer:

| | Question |
|:-:|:--|
| Q1 | Do FILAH's and TROUT's own datasets support their accusations? |
| Q2 | Using the full journalist dataset, is the COOTEFOO board actually biased? |
| Q3 | Are the accusations strengthened or weakened when compared against the full dataset? |
| Q4 | How does an individual member's story change depending on which dataset you use? |

---

## 2. Data Preparation

### From knowledge graph to relational tables

The raw data is structured as a knowledge graph — nodes represent entities (people, organisations, topics, meetings, places, trips) and edges represent connections between them. Each edge has a `role` attribute describing the type of connection: `participant`, `about`, `part_of`, `plan`, `refers_to`, or `travel`.

For Tableau, this was flattened into relational tables. The journalist links file (2,436 rows) became the base table. FILAH links (765 rows) and TROUT links (378 rows) were left-joined onto this base using a three-field composite key: `source + target + role`.

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
A naive union of all three files produces triple-counting because every FILAH and TROUT row already exists in the journalist file. The join approach preserves exactly **2,436 rows** while tagging each row with boolean flags (`In FILAH`, `In TROUT`) indicating which groups included it.
:::

The journalist nodes file (740 rows) was added as two separate related tables — Target Nodes and Source Nodes — to enable attribute lookups on both ends of each link. A geo nodes file (2,664 rows) was related separately for geographic enrichment of travel links.

### Cleaning and assumptions

Several data quality issues required attention:

| Issue | Resolution |
|:--|:--|
| `industry` and `short_topic` columns exist but are empty | Topic classification reconstructed from `source` field using `STARTSWITH()` pattern matching against 15 known topic slugs |
| `sentiment` stored as a string with literal "NA" values | `FLOAT()` conversion applied, returning NULL for non-numeric values |
| Sentiment scores only exist on `participant` role links | All sentiment analysis filtered to this role type (218 of 2,436 rows) |
| Geographic node IDs are numeric strings | Explicit string type enforcement applied on both sides of the geo relationship |

### Key structural decision: joins not unions

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
Using Tableau's **physical layer joins** rather than a union was the core architectural decision. Joins allow the dataset membership flags to be computed at row level, enabling any chart to simulate the FILAH, TROUT, or Full Journalist perspective through a single filter switch — without maintaining three separate data sources.
:::

---

## 3. Analytical Approach

### Overlap analysis

Before building any visualisations, we verified the relationship between the three datasets programmatically.

::: {.callout-important appearance="minimal"}
**Key finding:** All 765 FILAH rows and all 378 TROUT rows are exact subsets of the 2,436 journalist rows. Both activist groups drew from the same evidence pool. This structural finding is the foundation for every analytical claim in the project.
:::

### Sentiment analysis

Sentiment scores (ranging from −1 to +1) on participant links capture how board members engaged with each discussion topic. We computed mean sentiment by industry and by member, across each dataset view. The divergence between FILAH and TROUT views — and the convergence toward balance in the full dataset — is the primary quantitative evidence of sampling bias.

### Topic coverage analysis

We mapped which of the 15 known discussion topics appeared in each dataset. Two topics were completely absent from one group's data:

::: {layout-ncol=2}
::: {}
`concert` (Tourism topic)  
→ **Excluded by FILAH**  
Present in TROUT and Journalist
:::
::: {}
`seafood_festival` (Fishing topic)  
→ **Excluded by TROUT**  
Present in FILAH and Journalist
:::
:::

This selective omission is direct evidence of deliberate curation rather than innocent sampling.

### Member inclusion analysis

FILAH recorded only 3 of 6 members. TROUT recorded a different 3 of 6. One member — Tante Titan — appeared in neither group's dataset, only in the journalist's additional records. The selection pattern is not random: each group chose the members whose records best supported their narrative.

### Geographic / travel analysis

Travel links connect board members' activities to physical locations with zone classifications (industrial, tourism, residential, government, commercial). FILAH recorded 15 of 22 travel links, predominantly to industrial zones. TROUT recorded only 4 of 22, suppressing nearly all geographic evidence.

### Comparison framework

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
All comparisons between datasets use **proportions, not raw counts**, because dataset sizes differ substantially (FILAH: 765, TROUT: 378, Journalist: 2,436). Every cross-dataset chart uses either normalised values or clearly labelled absolute counts with N displayed.
:::

---

## 4. Visualisation Design Decisions

### Chart type rationale

| Chart type | Where used | Why |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Bar chart (trellis) | Sentiment by industry, three dataset panels | Directly comparable across panels when axes are locked to the same range (−1 to 1). Side-by-side panels make the FILAH/TROUT/Journalist divergence immediately legible. |
| Heatmap | Topic coverage across datasets | The primary value is making absent cells visible. A heatmap makes zero counts and missing topics visually obvious at a glance. |
| Diverging stacked bar | Board-wide positive vs negative engagement | Answers Q2 directly by showing the ratio of positive to negative interactions per industry. |
| Dot plot | Member sentiment by industry, per dataset panel | Handles the sparse structure well — NULLs render as absence rather than zero. |
| Bar chart (stacked, sorted) | Member participation counts by industry | Shows both volume (total bar length) and composition (colour segments) simultaneously. |
| Heatmap (member × topic) | Member engagement across all topics | Two dimensions (members, topics) with colour encoding turns a 6×15 matrix into a scannable pattern. |
| Bar chart (grouped) | Accusations vs Reality | Six bars in two industry groups comparing partial claims against the full dataset baseline. |
| Bar chart + dot plot (parameter-driven) | Member spotlight | Parameter controls allow the journalist to select any member and dataset view interactively. |
| Bar chart (travel destinations) | Travel zone by dataset | Raw counts by destination zone across three panels — honest at this sample size (22 links). |

### Trade-offs and simplifications

::: {.callout-caution appearance="minimal" collapse="true"}
**Sentiment as a proxy for bias.**
Sentiment scores were assigned in the original dataset and we treat them as given. We did not verify the scoring methodology. Sentiment is used as an indicator of engagement quality, not as a definitive measure of bias.

**No network visualisation.**
The data is structured as a knowledge graph. However, for the specific analytical questions posed (sentiment comparison, topic coverage, member inclusion), tabular aggregations in Tableau proved more analytically tractable than network diagrams. A network view would show connectivity but not the quantitative divergence between datasets that is the core finding.

**15 topics only.**
Our topic classification covers the 15 named topics visible in the journalist nodes file. Any discussion nodes that did not match a known topic prefix are classified as NULL and excluded from industry-level analysis.

**Tableau relationships vs joins.**
We chose physical joins for the three links tables rather than Tableau relationships. Relationships would have prevented cross-table row-level calculations — specifically, the boolean membership flags that underpin all dataset comparison charts cannot be computed when source tables are in different relationship contexts.
:::
